title: The UN Architecture — Why the World Built This
aiPrompt: Explain the UN system to me as a 14-year-old beginner — the GA, the Security Council, ECOSOC, and Crisis Committees — and quiz me on 3 core differences.
dailyRitual: Watch one 10-min UN history clip. Sketch the 6 principal organs on paper from memory. Run the AI quiz. Score yourself out of 3.
winningEdge: Winners can name which committee a real-world headline belongs to within 5 seconds. Build that reflex on Day 1, not Day 30.

title: Rules of Procedure — The Invisible Operating System
aiPrompt: Teach me Moderated Caucus vs Unmoderated Caucus vs Formal Debate, with 1 example of when each is strategically used. Then quiz me with 5 RoP scenarios.
dailyRitual: Memorise 6 procedural motions in order of disruptiveness. Practise raising each one out loud to a mirror. Speak with conviction, not apology.
winningEdge: The delegate who controls the motion controls the room. RoP fluency is the cheapest competitive edge in MUN — most delegates fake it.

title: Country Profile — Becoming Your Nation
aiPrompt: I'm representing [Country] on [Agenda]. Give me a cheat sheet of our top 3 allies, top 2 adversaries, key trade dependencies, and our last 3 UN votes on this issue.
dailyRitual: Print a one-page country card: flag, GDP, head of state, two red lines, two green lights. Carry it in your pocket for the next 27 days.
winningEdge: Never break character. The moment you argue what you believe instead of what your country believes, the chair sees through you. Diplomacy is acting school with footnotes.

title: Agenda Decoding — Reading the Brief Like a Lawyer
aiPrompt: Break my committee agenda into 5 sub-issues. For each sub-issue, tell me what my country gains, what it loses, and one strategic ambiguity I can exploit.
dailyRitual: Highlight your study guide in 3 colours: green (we win here), red (we must defend), yellow (negotiate). No paragraph left uncoloured.
winningEdge: The agenda is never one question. It is five smaller questions hidden inside one big one. Award-winning delegates fight on the sub-issue where their country is strongest.

title: The 60-Second Opening — Your First Impression
aiPrompt: Review my 60-second opening speech. Check for policy contradictions, weak hooks, and filler words. Rewrite the first sentence in 2 more commanding versions.
dailyRitual: Record yourself 3 times. Listen back at 1.5x speed — if you can't follow yourself, the chair can't either. Cut every 'um', 'so', 'basically'.
winningEdge: First 7 seconds = the entire impression. Open with a date, a statistic, or a question. Never open with 'Honourable chair, fellow delegates' — everyone does that.

title: Voice, Posture, Eye Contact — Diplomatic Presence
aiPrompt: Listen to my recorded speech. Grade me on pacing, modulation, pauses, and confidence. Give me one drill for each weakness.
dailyRitual: Stand up, plant feet shoulder-width, lower the voice by one note. Re-record the same speech. Compare. Pick the better take as your baseline.
winningEdge: The delegate who pauses owns the room. Two seconds of silence after a powerful line beats ten minutes of fast talking. Chairs notice composure first, content second.

title: Week 1 Review — Foundations Locked
aiPrompt: Run a 10-question diagnostic across UN structure, RoP, my country profile, and speech mechanics. Grade me out of 100 and identify my single weakest area.
dailyRitual: Rest for 30 minutes. Then take the diagnostic. Write your weak area on a Post-it and stick it on your desk for Week 2.
winningEdge: Reflection is a competitive weapon. Most delegates train forward only. Champions audit themselves every 7 days. That's how plateau becomes progression.

title: Position Paper — The Skeleton
aiPrompt: Outline a 3-section position paper for me: (1) country background on the issue, (2) past UN action, (3) proposed solutions. Use my country's actual diplomatic language.
dailyRitual: Draft only the skeleton — bullet points, no full sentences. Save it as v0.1. Resist the urge to polish today.
winningEdge: Pros draft skeleton first, prose second. Amateurs write prose first and end up with beautiful sentences that contradict each other.

title: Position Paper — The Evidence Layer
aiPrompt: For each bullet in my outline, give me one citation (UN resolution number, treaty article, or recent statistic) my country would actually cite.
dailyRitual: Stuff each section with 2 verifiable citations. UN docs only — no Wikipedia. Use the official UN Digital Library and your country's foreign ministry site.
winningEdge: One real resolution number beats ten elegant adjectives. Chairs award delegates who quote A/RES/77/281 by reference, not delegates who say 'as the UN has said many times.'

title: Position Paper — The Diplomatic Voice
aiPrompt: Rewrite my paper in formal diplomatic register. Replace casual phrasing with treaty language. Flag any sentence where my country would never say that.
dailyRitual: Read your paper out loud as if you were a foreign minister at a press conference. If it sounds like a school essay, rewrite. If it sounds like a press release, keep.
winningEdge: Submit your paper 48 hours before deadline. Chairs read papers in the order received. Late papers are skimmed. Early papers earn early respect.

title: Motion Strategy — Steering the Agenda
aiPrompt: Main agenda: [X]. Give me 5 strategic Moderated Caucus topics that favour my country's interests. Rank them by how likely they are to pass a vote.
dailyRitual: Write each motion on a card. Practise raising the motion in 12 words or fewer. Long motions die in committee.
winningEdge: He who frames the sub-topic wins the debate before it begins. If the committee debates 'climate finance' instead of 'climate refugees', you've already decided who looks responsible.

title: Speech Bank — Building Reusable Arguments
aiPrompt: For my country, generate 8 reusable 90-second speech modules — one for each likely Mod Caucus topic. Each must have a hook, a statistic, and a proposed action.
dailyRitual: Store the 8 modules in a Notes app folder titled 'Mod Caucus Bank'. Re-read on the bus to committee. You will use 5 of them on Day 1 of any conference.
winningEdge: Top delegates do not improvise — they recombine pre-built modules in real time. Improvisation is what you do when your prep fails. Prep wins gavels.

title: Active Listening — The Note-Taking System
aiPrompt: Teach me a 4-column note system: Speaker | Country | Key Claim | My Rebuttal. Then drill me with 5 mock speeches and check my notes.
dailyRitual: Practise with a 6-minute YouTube clip of a real UN GA speech. Take notes in the 4-column system. Compare with the AI's transcript.
winningEdge: While other delegates check their phones during speeches, you take rebuttal notes. By the third hour of committee, you have a fully loaded weapon and they have nothing.

title: Rebuttal Drills — The Counter-Punch
aiPrompt: Play the delegate of [adversary country]. Make your harshest 60-second argument against my country's position. I'll respond. Grade my rebuttal on 3 criteria: directness, evidence, and tone.
dailyRitual: Run 5 rounds. Three with the AI playing your direct adversary. Two with the AI playing a 'concerned ally' — those are harder.
winningEdge: Never rebut emotionally. Always start a rebuttal with: 'The delegate of X raises an important point — however, the data shows...' You concede nothing and earn credibility.

title: Bloc Mapping — Who Are Your People?
aiPrompt: Map my country into 3 possible blocs on this agenda. For each bloc, name 4 likely member countries, the shared interest, and the natural bloc leader.
dailyRitual: Draw the bloc map by hand. Three circles, names inside, lines connecting allies. Stick on your wall. This is your war map.
winningEdge: Most delegates wait for a bloc to form around them. Winners walk into the unmod with a bloc map already drawn — and walk out as the bloc leader.

title: The Unmoderated Caucus — Social Engineering
aiPrompt: Roleplay: I'm trying to convince a stubborn major power to join my bloc. You play the major power. Resist for 5 turns. After, grade my negotiation tactics.
dailyRitual: Practise the 'sandwich technique': open with their interest, insert your ask, close with shared credit. Run this 3 times with the AI.
winningEdge: Unmods are won by who listens most, not who talks loudest. Walk into the unmod with 3 questions ready. Whoever asks the best questions ends up writing the resolution.

title: Working Paper Architecture
aiPrompt: Turn these 6 bullet ideas into a working paper structure: title, signatories, 4 preambulatory clauses, 6 operative clauses. Use UN-compliant verbs.
dailyRitual: Memorise 10 preambulatory openers (Recalling, Affirming, Noting with concern...) and 10 operative verbs (Calls upon, Decides, Urges, Requests...). Flashcards on phone.
winningEdge: The first delegate to circulate a working paper becomes the de facto bloc author. Even a mediocre draft circulated on Day 1 beats a brilliant draft circulated on Day 2.

title: Operative Clauses — The Verb Game
aiPrompt: Rewrite my 6 operative clauses. Replace weak verbs (encourages, suggests) with strong ones (decides, demands) — but only where my country's actual mandate allows. Flag overreach.
dailyRitual: Print your clauses. Circle every verb. Audit: does this country actually have the authority for this verb in this committee?
winningEdge: Security Council can 'decide'. General Assembly can only 'recommend'. ECOSOC can 'urge'. Using the wrong verb is the fastest way to get marked as a beginner.

title: Merger Strategy — Combining Papers
aiPrompt: I have my draft. Bloc B has a competing draft. Identify 4 clauses we could merge, 2 we must keep separate, and the one concession that would seal the deal.
dailyRitual: Practise the merger pitch in 60 seconds. Open with what their paper does well. Then propose the merger. Close with shared sponsorship credit.
winningEdge: Don't fight to win — fight to merge on your terms. A merged resolution with your top 3 operative clauses intact is a bigger gavel-magnet than a solo paper that gets defeated.

title: Amendment Warfare
aiPrompt: Take each operative clause in my draft. Tell me how a hostile delegate would attack it — semantic loopholes, funding gaps, sovereignty concerns. Then give me a 30-second defence for each.
dailyRitual: Memorise the defence for your top 3 clauses verbatim. When attacked, deliver the defence in 30 seconds without checking notes.
winningEdge: Friendly amendments strengthen your paper and bring co-sponsors. Hostile amendments kill it. Learn to convert hostile into friendly with one sentence.

title: Week 3 Review — Coalition Builder Locked
aiPrompt: Run a coalition-building scenario. I'm in unmod, my bloc has 6 countries, an adversary is stealing 2 members. Simulate 8 turns. Coach me through.
dailyRitual: After the scenario, list 3 things you did well and 1 thing you'd redo. This is your debrief muscle — you'll use it after every real committee session.
winningEdge: Three weeks in, you are now in the top 20% of any high-school MUN room. The next 9 days move you into the top 5% — the gavel zone.

title: Crisis Committees 101 — Speed Over Polish
aiPrompt: Explain how a crisis committee differs from GA: portfolio powers, directives, joint vs individual, press leaks. Then give me 3 sample crisis updates and the optimal first move for each.
dailyRitual: Practise writing a directive in under 4 minutes: header, action verbs, signatures. Time yourself. Repeat until you hit 4 minutes consistently.
winningEdge: Crisis is won by decisiveness, not perfection. A 70%-good directive submitted first beats a 95%-perfect directive submitted third. Speed = leverage.

title: Portfolio Powers — Knowing What You Own
aiPrompt: I'm playing [Crisis Character]. List my full portfolio powers (military, economic, diplomatic, personal). Then test me with 3 scenarios where I must use exactly one power.
dailyRitual: Write your portfolio powers on a card. Read it 3 times before any crisis session. Most delegates forget they have a navy.
winningEdge: In crisis, your portfolio powers ARE your character. Delegates who pull obscure but real powers at the right moment get remembered by the dais forever.

title: The Midnight Crisis Drill
aiPrompt: Inject a sudden 2 AM crisis into my current agenda — choose between a coup, a market crash, a cyberattack, or a leaked document. Give me 3 minutes. Then grade my directive on speed, plausibility, and strategic upside.
dailyRitual: Run this drill 3 times today. Different crisis each time. Notice your patterns — do you default to military? Try a diplomatic move next round.
winningEdge: Win the night, win the gavel. Crisis chairs remember who was alert at 11pm session 4. That's where awards are decided, not in the morning sessions.

title: Press, Leaks & Communiqués
aiPrompt: Draft me 3 press communiqués from my character: (1) public statement after a controversial vote, (2) backchannel note to a rival, (3) leak to the international press. Match tone to audience.
dailyRitual: Read all 3 aloud. The public statement should sound official. The backchannel should sound human. The leak should sound dangerous. If they all sound the same, redraft.
winningEdge: Three voices, one delegate. Top crisis players write differently for different audiences. Same person, different mask — that's diplomacy.

title: Full Committee Simulation — Round 1
aiPrompt: Simulate a 6-turn Security Council debate on cybersecurity. You play the chair plus 3 opposing delegates. Call on me, challenge me, advance the clock. After, give me a written feedback report.
dailyRitual: Do this in one sitting, no breaks. Stand up to speak. Sit down to vote. Treat it like the real room. Print the feedback report and underline 1 line to fix.
winningEdge: Simulation > revision. One full mock committee teaches you more than 10 hours of reading. Run two more before Day 30 — three full mocks is the minimum competitive prep.

title: Full Committee Simulation — Round 2 (Crisis)
aiPrompt: Now run a 6-turn crisis simulation. Inject 2 crisis updates mid-flow. You play chair plus 4 characters. After, grade me on adaptability, portfolio use, and directive quality.
dailyRitual: Compare today's report with yesterday's. Same weaknesses? You have a pattern. Different weaknesses? You're growing. Either way, write the fix on tomorrow's to-do.
winningEdge: Patterns reveal personality, and personality is what chairs award. Find your archetype — fast strategist or consensus builder — and double down.

title: Voice, Pacing & Final Polish
aiPrompt: Listen to my final opening speech recording. Analyse tone, pacing, emphasis, confidence markers, and filler words. Score me out of 100 and tell me exactly 3 things to fix before conference day.
dailyRitual: Record the opening once. Apply the 3 fixes. Record again. Compare. The second recording is the one you take to the conference.
winningEdge: On conference day, the opening speech is the only part you have full control over. Memorise it. Own it. Everything after that is improvisation.

title: Conference Day Logistics — The Boring Critical List
aiPrompt: Generate a conference-day checklist for me: documents to print, dress code, timing, backup plans, what to eat, what to carry, what to say in the first 30 seconds of unmod.
dailyRitual: Print the checklist. Pack the bag tonight. Lay out the formal attire. Set two alarms. This sounds excessive — that's the point.
winningEdge: Awards are lost to forgotten placards and dead phone batteries. Logistics is invisible until it fails. Pros over-prepare boring things so they can be loose with creative things.

title: Gavel Mindset — The Final Calibration
aiPrompt: Final dress rehearsal. Listen to my full opening speech and my 60-second crisis response. Give me a final score and one sentence I should repeat to myself walking into the conference.
dailyRitual: Go for a 15-minute walk. No phone. Rehearse the opening line in your head. Imagine the chair calling your country. Sit down. You're ready.
winningEdge: On Day 30, you have done more deliberate practice than 90% of delegates in any room you'll enter. You are not hoping for a gavel — you are arriving to collect one.
